prompt: Each dot is a Galapagos island in predictor space (area and elevation). Tap the high-leverage island: the one sitting alone, far from the crowd.
why: Leverage h_ii is the diagonal of the hat matrix; it measures how far a case's predictors sit from the center of the predictor cloud, and says nothing about the response (Section 9.1, @ch09-leverage). Isabela, at area 4669, is far larger than any other island, so its predictor row is unlike the rest and its leverage is h = 0.9685, past the 2p/n = 0.40 cutoff. A point out here forces the fit to follow it regardless of its species count.

prompt: In the gala fit (n = 30, p = 6) the leverage values sum to 6.0, and Isabela has h = 0.9685, above the 2p/n = 0.40 cutoff. Which reading is correct?
options: Isabela is fit badly, since a high leverage value means a large residual.
Isabela sits in an unusual part of predictor space, so it has the potential to distort the fit.
The leverage values summing to 6 shows the model explains 6 percent of the variation.
A leverage value above 0.40 proves Isabela should be deleted.
why: A leverage value is a statement about the predictors only: it measures potential to influence, not whether the response is fit well or badly (Section 9.1, @ch09-leverage). The values sum to p = 6 because tr(H) = p, not because of any R-squared. High leverage is a reason to look closer, never an automatic reason to delete; whether Isabela actually distorts the fit is the separate influence question of Section 9.3.

prompt: One sentence about the four residual scales is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: The raw residual is e_i = Y_i - Yhat_i, the part of Y_i the fit missed.
A high-leverage point has a LARGER raw-residual variance, so its residual looks too big.
Var{e_i} = sigma^2 (1 - h_ii), so dividing by sqrt(1 - h_ii) puts residuals on one scale.
The studentized deleted residual t_i uses s_(i), the error SD estimated without case i.

why: Theorem 9.5 gives Var{e_i} = sigma^2 (1 - h_ii), so a high-leverage point has SMALLER residual variance, not larger: the fit bends to meet it, shrinking its raw residual and hiding trouble (Section 9.2, @ch09-studentized). That is exactly why you divide by sqrt(1 - h_ii) to restore a common scale, and why the studentized deleted residual uses s_(i) to avoid a real outlier masking itself.

prompt: In the gala model Isabela's studentized deleted residual is -5.33, and the Bonferroni cutoff is t(1 - 0.05/(2n); n-p-1) = 3.56. What do you conclude?
options: Nothing: 5.33 is negative, so the test does not apply.
Isabela is a genuine statistical outlier, since |-5.33| exceeds the 3.56 cutoff.
Isabela is fine, because -5.33 is less than 3.56.
The cutoff 3.56 means 3.56 percent of islands are outliers.
why: You compare the largest |t_i| to the Bonferroni cutoff t(1 - alpha/(2n); n-p-1) (Theorem 9.6, Section 9.2, @ch09-studentized). Here |-5.33| = 5.33 is far past 3.56, so Isabela is fit far worse than any clean model would produce: a real outlier, not just an unusual predictor row. The Bonferroni divisor of 2n corrects for scanning all n cases for the most extreme one, holding the family-wide false-alarm rate below alpha.

prompt: Order the steps of the Bonferroni test for a single outlier.
steps: Compute the studentized deleted residual t_i for every case
Find the most extreme case: the one with the largest |t_i|
Build the Bonferroni cutoff t(1 - alpha/(2n); n - p - 1)
Compare that largest |t_i| to the cutoff
Flag the case as an outlier only if its |t_i| exceeds the cutoff
why: Because you single out the most extreme of n cases, you are implicitly running n tests, so the cutoff must be corrected (Section 9.2, @ch09-studentized). You compute all t_i, take the largest in absolute value, and test it against t(1 - alpha/(2n); n - p - 1); the 2n divisor keeps the chance of even one false alarm across all cases below alpha. For gala this flags Isabela at -5.33 against 3.56.

prompt: Match each influence measure to what it summarizes.

pairs.meaning: how far ALL fitted values move when case i is deleted
the change in case i's OWN fitted value, rescaled
the change in ONE coefficient b_k, rescaled
the leverage factor inside Cook's distance

why: Every influence measure combines a residual with the leverage value (Section 9.3, @ch09-cooks-distance). Cook's distance D_i = (r_i^2/p) h_ii/(1 - h_ii) tracks the whole vector of fitted values; DFFITS localizes to case i's own fitted value; DFBETAS localizes to a single coefficient. The factor h_ii/(1 - h_ii) is the common leverage term that makes influence explode as h_ii approaches 1.

prompt: The savings fit gives Zambia (h = 0.064, studentized residual 2.85, D = 0.097) and Libya (h = 0.531, studentized residual -1.09, D = 0.268). One sentence is wrong. Tap it.

sentences.text: Zambia has the largest studentized residual, so it is fit worst by the model.
Zambia is the country most distorting the fit, because its residual is the largest.
Libya has the highest leverage value and the largest Cook's distance, 0.268.
Influence needs both an unusual predictor row and a poorly fit response, not either alone.

why: A large residual is not the same as influence (Definition 9.7, Section 9.3, @ch09-cooks-distance). Zambia is fit poorly but sits at an ordinary leverage value (h = 0.064), so the crowd holds the line in place and its Cook's distance is only 0.097. Libya, with the highest leverage value (h = 0.531), bends the fit the most: its Cook's distance 0.268 is the largest in the data. The right question is never who is fit worst, but who would change the answer if they left.
